title: NBFC Analyst Handbook v2: RBI's Nov 2025 Master Directions Framework
executive_summary: 38-chapter credit-analyst reference built on RBI's 28 Nov 2025 overhaul, which split the consolidated 2023 SBR Master Direction into 36 themed MDs while preserving the substance: the four-layer SBR pyramid (BL/ML/UL/TL), 15% CRAR / 10% Tier-1 / 9% CET-1 for UL, the NPA glide path to 90 days by 31 Mar 2026, and 25%/40% Tier-1 concentration limits. Sector context: ~9,300 NBFCs, ~Rs 47 lakh crore assets, ~23% of systemic credit, only ~25 deposit-takers.

Foundations (Ch 1-6): the 50-50 Principal Business Criteria (financial assets >50% of total assets AND financial income >50% of gross income) determines NBFC status; entry NOF is Rs 10 cr (legacy glide path Rs 5 cr by Mar 2025, Rs 10 cr by Mar 2027); Public Funds is defined inclusively (ICDs, bank finance, CPs — everything except <=5-year compulsorily convertibles); NOF deducts group exposures above 10% of Owned Fund; ML classification triggers at Rs 1,000 cr assets (aggregated across group NBFCs, real-time, one-way sticky), UL is top ~25 by RBI scoring with mandatory listing in 3 years and a 5-year sticky period.

Prudential core (Ch 7-10): BL runs on a 7x leverage cap; ML/UL on 15% CRAR with gold-loan NBFCs at 12% Tier-1; PDI capped at 15% of Tier-1; unsecured consumer credit and credit cards carry 125% risk weight (Nov 2023 hike preserved); NPA recognition is day-end with cross-default contamination and upgrade only on full arrears clearance; provisioning runs 0.25-0.40% standard (UL differential: 2% teaser housing, 1% CRE) through 10% sub-standard to 100% loss; Ind-AS NBFCs apply ECL with an IRACP floor via a non-distributable Impairment Reserve.

New themed MDs (Ch 11-15): Concentration Risk Mgmt extends Board-policy discipline to BL and gives UL a bank-style Large Exposure Framework (LE at >=10% Tier-1, aggregate cap 800% of Tier-1); Credit Risk Mgmt consolidates lending restrictions (75% gold LTV, 50% share LTV, Rs 1 cr IPO cap, top-ups on depreciating assets treated unsecured, AIF-debtor liquidation in 30 days or 100% provision, LEI mandatory above Rs 50 cr); Resolution of Stressed Assets hardwires the 30-day Review Period, ICA at 75% by value/60% by number, 180-day implementation deadline and 20%+15% additional provisioning; Transfer & Co-lending MD sets MHP 3/6 months, MRR 5%/10%, and the 80:20 co-lending model with FLDG capped at 5%.

Governance and conduct (Ch 16-19): ML/UL need ACB, NRC, RMC (RMC now for all NBFCs), CCO (15 years experience, 3-year tenure), CRO above Rs 50 cr, RBIA above Rs 5,000 cr, joint audit above Rs 15,000 cr, ICAAP with climate risk; the Responsible Business Conduct MD consolidates FPC, mandatory KFS with APR (since 1 Oct 2024), penal charges replacing penal interest, interest only from disbursement, 30-day property-document return with Rs 5,000/day penalty; deposit-takers face 1.5x NOF cap (3x for HFCs), 12-60 month tenors and a 15% liquid-asset reserve with no deposit insurance.

Specialised entities (Ch 20-22): microfinance is activity-based — collateral-free loans to households earning <=Rs 3 lakh with a 50% FOIR ceiling across ALL household debt; NBFC-MFIs need >=75% qualifying assets; 2024-25 sector stress pushed some MFI GNPAs from ~2% to 6%+. CICs need >=90% group assets/>=60% group equity, 30% ANW ratio, 2.5x leverage, two-layer cap — rating them is group-cash-flow analysis. HFCs need 60% housing assets with 50% individual housing loans, LTV caps 90/80/75% by ticket size.

Crime, reporting, IT (Ch 23-28): post-Rajesh Agarwal SC ruling, fraud and wilful-defaulter classification require SCN, 21-day response and reasoned order; fraud debars for 5 years post-repayment, WD (>=Rs 25 lakh) bars fresh credit, capital markets (5 years) and IBC 29A; WD regime extends to ML+ NBFCs from 2024; CIC reporting fortnightly from 1 Jan 2025; CRILC at Rs 5 cr aggregate exposure with weekly SMA-2 reporting; NBS-2 auditor certificate and DNBS returns are the regulator's ground truth; IT Governance MD mandates CIO+independent CISO, ITSC, SOC and CFSS rollout (100% by 30 Sep 2025 for ML/UL with >=10 FPSDUs).

Extended coverage (Ch 29-38): a seven-risk taxonomy and 5C synthesis frame the rating lens; the brand-new Climate Finance MD adds Board climate-risk ownership and a Green Deposits framework (third-party verified, HQLA-parked pending allocation); Securitisation MD adds Basel STC treatment (senior RW from 10% vs 15%) with retained tranches up to 1,250% RW; project finance carries 1% construction-phase provisioning and DCCO extension limits (4/3/2/1 years); PCE capped ~20% of issue size; Dividends MD caps payouts at 50% (60% CIC/SPD) only with 3-year CRAR compliance and NNPA <6%, else 10% cap; the Financial Statements MD standardises 45+ disclosure tables with quarterly disclosure from Oct 2025, making peer comparison newly tractable.

key_takeaways.takeaway: The Nov 2025 overhaul is architectural, not a tightening — all binding prudential numbers (15% CRAR, Rs 1,000 cr ML threshold, 90-day NPA, concentration caps) are preserved, so no rating actions flow from the re-issuance itself, but committee citations must now reference the specific themed MD, not the repealed 2023 SBR MD.
key_takeaways.analyst_lens: Continuity of obligations is saved by transition clauses; the credit-relevant change is eight new/consolidated themed regimes (Climate, RBC, Concentration, Credit Risk, Resolution, ALM, Transfer, Investment Portfolio). Track issuer compliance gap analyses against the new instruments in FY27 disclosures.

key_takeaways.takeaway: Layer placement drives everything: a BL NBFC approaching Rs 1,000 cr (standalone or group-aggregated) faces a step-change to 15% CRAR, CCO/CRO/ICAAP and statutory concentration caps — a fast-growing thin-capital BL is riskier than its current layer suggests.
key_takeaways.analyst_lens: Classification is real-time, aggregated across group NBFCs, and one-way sticky. Track asset size vs the Rs 1,000 cr threshold plus pro-forma CRAR under ML rules 6-12 months ahead; also flag the LCR trigger at Rs 5,000 cr. The 'split-the-book' gambit fails on group aggregation.

key_takeaways.takeaway: Headline capital ratios overstate loss absorption: PDI can be 15% of Tier-1, revaluation reserves enter at 55% discount, DTA rules bite CET-1, and NOF deducts group exposures above 10% of Owned Fund — always compute equity-only Tier-1 and NOF, not reported net worth.
key_takeaways.analyst_lens: A 15.5% CRAR with 12% Tier-1 can hide pure-equity Tier-1 near 9%. Watch for PDI top-ups at quarter-end and group-exposure shuffling between reporting dates. Single metric: equity-only Tier-1/RWA, especially for UL where CET-1 >=9% is the binding quality floor.

key_takeaways.takeaway: Asset-quality mechanics are unforgiving by design: day-end NPA recognition, cross-default across all facilities of a borrower, upgrade only on full arrears clearance, and restructured accounts held sub-standard for 12 months — a rising 'restructured standard' book is the classic evergreening flag.
key_takeaways.analyst_lens: SMA-2 stock is next quarter's GNPA; Stage 1-to-2 migration leads the GNPA print by 2-3 quarters for Ind-AS issuers. Adjust Ind-AS vs IGAAP peers (Stage 3 + Stage 2 30+ DPD) before comparing. Track roll rates (SMA-2 to NPA) and the one-year restructured-vintage flow back to NPA — if oddly low, dig.

key_takeaways.takeaway: The Resolution MD gives stress-recognition real teeth: default triggers a 30-day Review Period, ICA (75% value/60% number), 180-day implementation, and failure costs 20% then a further 15% additional provisioning — a direct, dated capital hit on every large stressed exposure.
key_takeaways.analyst_lens: An 'Additional Specific Provisioning' line in financials means resolution deadlines were missed — identify the exposures. For project finance, serial DCCO extensions (limits: 4/3/2/1 years by project type) are the strongest single early-warning signal; beyond them the account turns sub-standard.

key_takeaways.takeaway: Co-lending, securitisation and DLG structures make off-balance-sheet risk the new blind spot: net capital release from securitisation is far smaller than headline de-recognition (retained tranches at up to 1,250% RW), FLDG is capped at 5% but implicit layering exists, and undrawn committed lines consume RWA via 20-50% CCFs.
key_takeaways.analyst_lens: Rate the NBFC on full origination volume and operational risk, not the retained book. Reconcile capital x CRAR = implied RWA against on+off-BS exposure; check bank-partner concentration (1-2 partners can choke growth overnight), liquidity-facility draws to SPEs, and post-issuance implicit support (a regulatory violation that voids true sale).

key_takeaways.takeaway: Liquidity discipline post-IL&FS is codified — LCR >=100% for deposit-takers and non-deposit NBFCs >=Rs 5,000 cr — but headline LCR can mask granular refinance cliffs; a single unconfirmed large CP maturity inside 60 days can gut an apparently comfortable cushion.
key_takeaways.analyst_lens: Track LCR trend over 4 quarters (350% falling to 150% is a signal even above floor), CP >25% of borrowings (yellow) / >35% (red), top-5 lenders >40% of borrowings, and negative cumulative gaps in 1-30 day buckets. For NBFC-Ds, deposit renewal rates below ~50% signal strain with no deposit insurance to slow a run.

key_takeaways.takeaway: Microfinance is regulated activity-based with a 50% household FOIR cap on ALL debt (income <=Rs 3 lakh), and the 2024-25 stress — GNPA jumping from ~2% to 6%+ at some NBFC-MFIs on over-leverage and political waiver cycles — shows why FOIR integrity and state concentration are the binding rating variables.
key_takeaways.analyst_lens: CIC data plus self-declaration under-captures household debt, so FOIR breaches surface only in stress. Track state-wise exposure (2-3 state concentration is common), collection efficiency vs DPD divergence, and whether Board FOIR policy is conservative (40%) vs the 50% statutory max. Fortnightly CIC reporting since 1 Jan 2025 improves visibility.

key_takeaways.takeaway: Conduct and governance failures are the fastest route to a rating cap: KYC penalties run Rs 25 lakh-2 cr per finding, the RBC MD imposes borrower-level penalties (Rs 5,000/day for late property-document return), and fraud/WD classification now requires due process but still means 5-year funding debarment and IBC 29A bar for borrowers.
key_takeaways.analyst_lens: Vacant or dual-hatted CCO/CRO/CISO roles, auditor past rotation, growing related-party exposure and unclosed RBI observations are leading demerits. Cross-check the NBS-2 auditor certificate and DNBS-02/CRILC against issuer claims — regulator-vs-issuer inconsistency is a major rating signal.

key_takeaways.takeaway: The Dividends MD is a built-in weakness disclosure: full 50% payout (60% CIC/SPD) requires CRAR compliance and NNPA <6% in each of the last 3 years; an issuer capped at 10% payout is being force-retained by the regulator — read payout behaviour as a solvency signal.
key_takeaways.analyst_lens: The eligibility test converts capital/asset-quality history into an observable behaviour. A sudden payout jump to 50% can signal cyclical peak earnings or expected growth deceleration; a 10%-capped issuer failed the 3-year test. Track payout ratio vs the eligibility matrix annually alongside Section 45-IC statutory reserve transfers (20% of net profit).

entities_impacted.impact: Issued 36 themed Master Directions on 28 Nov 2025 replacing the 2023 consolidated SBR MD; preserves prudential substance while adding new regimes (Climate Finance, RBC, Concentration, Credit Risk, Resolution, ALM, Transfer & Co-lending, Investment Portfolio).

~9,300 entities with Rs 47 lakh crore assets funding 23% of systemic credit must re-map compliance to themed MDs; BL entities gain new obligations (RMC, concentration policy), ML/UL face full bank-aligned prudential, governance and IT load.

Top ~25 NBFCs face 9% CET-1, Large Exposure Framework (800% Tier-1 aggregate cap), differential standard-asset provisioning, bank-style compensation rules and mandatory listing within 3 years; sticky in UL for 5 years.

Activity-based regime: Rs 3 lakh household-income definition, 50% FOIR cap, 75% qualifying-asset test, fortnightly CIC reporting from Jan 2025; sector absorbing 2024-25 stress with GNPA rising from ~2% to 6%+ at some issuers.

Under RBI's HFC MD 2025: 60% housing / 50% individual-housing asset tests, LTV caps of 90/80/75% by ticket, Rs 20 cr NOF, 3x NOF deposit cap, differential provisioning (CRE 1%, teaser-rate housing 2%).

CIC-SI (>=Rs 100 cr with public funds) must hold ANW >=30% of RWA and leverage <=2.5x ANW with a two-layer stacking cap; 50% recognition of unrealised group-equity appreciation can flatter ANW, making group cash-flow analysis the rating crux.
~25 entities under the Public Deposits MD: 1.5x NOF deposit ceiling, 12-60 month tenors, 15% liquid-asset reserve, investment-grade rating requirement, no deposit insurance — run risk is structural.

entities_impacted.entity: Digital lending NBFCs and LSP/fintech partners
entities_impacted.impact: Credit Facilities MD locks in direct disbursal flows, LSP fee rules, cooling-off periods, and 5% cash/HQLA-only DLG cap; NBFC retains full accountability for KYC, recovery and data regardless of delegation.
Transfer & Distribution MD formalises the 80:20 co-lending model with single blended-rate KFS; bank partner exit or fee renegotiation is a concentrated business-model risk for originating NBFCs.

learning: Regulatory ratios are floors, not comfort: an issuer 'compliant but tight' on multiple metrics (24% single-borrower vs 25% cap, CRAR at 15.3%) is one bad quarter from cascading breaches — rate the aggregate margin of safety, not each ratio's pass/fail.

Triangulate three views of the same issuer: what management discloses, what the auditor certifies to RBI (NBS-2), and what the regulator sees (DNBS-02, CRILC SMA data). Inconsistencies between them are themselves the rating signal, often ahead of the financials.

Capital quality beats capital quantity: strip PDIs, revaluation reserves, DTAs and group double-counting to get equity-only Tier-1 and true NOF before comparing peers — the Rs 14 cr NOF haircut in a Rs 40 cr Owned Fund worked example shows how a model using headline net worth overstates buffers by a third.

Off-book is not off-risk: co-lending FLDGs, retained securitisation tranches, PCE, undrawn committed lines and letters of comfort all absorb capital or crystallise under stress; rate origination volume and contingent exposures, not just the balance-sheet loan book.
